intro:
The Books of Samuel (1–2 Samuel, originally one book) are anonymous, and the prophet whose name they bear cannot be their sole author: Samuel's death is recorded partway through the first book (1 Samuel 25:1), while the narrative continues to the end of David's reign.

The Chronicler reports that the acts of David were written ‘in the records of Samuel the seer, of Nathan the prophet, and of Gad the seer’ (1 Chronicles 29:29), and Jewish tradition accordingly names these three prophets as the contributing authors. The finished work is best understood as a later compilation that wove together such prophetic records.

sections.title: The prophetic records
sections.body: First Chronicles 29:29 preserves the memory that Samuel, Nathan, and Gad each kept written accounts of the events of their day. Whoever gave Samuel its final shape drew on these and other early sources — including David's own psalms, such as his ‘last words’ (2 Samuel 23:1) — to tell the story of Israel's transition from judges to monarchy.

sections.title: Why not Samuel alone
sections.body: Because Samuel dies before David is crowned, the books cannot be his unaided composition. The bulk of 2 Samuel — the ‘court history’ of David's reign — must come from later hands who had access to the royal court's records, leaving the work, like Kings, an anonymous prophetic history.
